paper.title: Emergent Mental Lexicon Functions in ChatGPT

sentences.text: LLMs have developed a surprising and impressive ability to perform a wide range of prompted tasks on which they were not directly trained (Wei, Tay, et al., 2022).
sentences.note: Asserts the existence and significance of a phenomenon to open the paper.

sentences.text: Training is primarily based on learning sequences through prediction in the context of surrounding tokens (e.g. words), and training data consists of many long sequences of text and symbols culled from various corpora.
sentences.note: Unpacks the mechanism behind [1]. Parent: [1] (emph).

sentences.text: After training, a prompt can be written to begin a context, and then an LLM like that used in ChatGPT can continue and expand the context by autoregressively predicting and producing subsequent tokens until an end of response is predicted.
sentences.note: Continues unpacking the mechanism; parent [1].

sentences.text: As a result, LLMs can generalize from their training data to produce responses to prompts that answer questions, perform tasks, solve problems, and generate plausible text and/or numbers given the context (OpenAI, 2023).
sentences.note: Citation-backed generalization about LLM capability.

sentences.text: Continuations can be sometimes bizarre, offensive, biased, untruthful, or otherwise unhelpful, so LLM responses are also fine-tuned to be more helpful and appropriate, often through reinforcement learning based on targeted sets of prompts and responses (Ouyang et al., 2022).
sentences.note: Qualifies/limits the capability in [4].

sentences.text: LLM functions assembled from prompts are often referred to as emergent because they are untrained and become available as training corpora and model parameters become larger (Bommasani et al., 2021).
sentences.note: Names the "emergent" construct used as analytical machinery.

sentences.text: Fine-tuning plays a role in shaping LLM responses, especially those targeted by human feedback, but this feedback is limited relative to the remarkable breadth and scope of emergent functionality.
sentences.note: Qualifies [6] by limiting fine-tuning's role relative to emergence.

sentences.text: Investigations are underway to understand how LLM functions emerge from transformer networks.
sentences.note: Reports current state of research activity.

sentences.text: One technique is to prompt them with examples of the desired function so the LLM may generalize by similarity and analogy (Wei, Tay, et al., 2022).
sentences.note: Example of the investigations in [8]. Parent: [8] (tempstat).

sentences.text: Such few shot learning protocols can be effective, but functions can also be assembled with no examples, so-called zero shot learning or reasoning (Kojima et al., 2022).
sentences.note: Citation-backed report of an established distinction (few-shot vs. zero-shot), independent of [9].

sentences.text: Few and zero shot learning suggests that transformer networks learn general-purpose representations that serve as pre-trained "building blocks" ready for on-the-fly assembly directed by prompting (Jiang & Bansal, 2021).
sentences.note: Interpretive extension ("suggests") drawn from [10]; parent [10] is primary, so valid. Parent: [10] (tempstat).

sentences.text: Emergent function stands in contrast with the idea that prompts serve as cues for locating and recalling functions in training data memorized in ChatGPT's weight matrices (Reynolds & McDonell, 2021).
sentences.note: Sets emergence against the competing memorization account.

sentences.text: Indeed, LLMs can memorize sequences from their training data (Carlini et al., 2022), and the function of memorization may be as emergent as any other LLM function (Biderman et al., 2023).
sentences.note: Citation-backed findings complicating the contrast in [12].

sentences.text: We aim to test emergence versus memorization as a window into how LLMs may serve as models of human language and cognition.
sentences.note: States the paper's overarching aim.

sentences.text: We investigate the emergence versus memorization hypotheses by prompting ChatGPT with tests of the human mental lexicon, which refers to a person's knowledge of, and facility with, the words of their language, including spelling, sound, meaning, and grammar (Aitchison, 2012).
sentences.note: Specifies the method/target construct for [14]'s aim.

sentences.text: The mental lexicon has long been a testbed for theoretical frameworks of cognition and information processing, most notably in debates between rule-based versus connectionist processing (Coltheart et al., 1993; Smolensky, 1988).
sentences.note: Independent claim about the field's history; not link:opening since it asserts content beyond naming a topic.

sentences.text: LLMs are founded on connectionist processing, but they offer a different theory of the human mental lexicon, and of human cognition more generally, compared with prior connectionist or rule-based theories.
sentences.note: Positions LLMs against the two camps named in [16].

sentences.text: For instance, the Dual-Route Cascade model (Coltheart et al., 2001) of word and nonword reading posits several rule-based mechanisms for mapping letters to sounds, accessing the stored meanings and sounds of words, and producing sequences of phonemes.
sentences.note: Concrete example illustrating the rule-based side of [17]'s contrast. Parent: [17] (contrast).

sentences.text: By contrast, the distributed connectionist (Plaut et al., 1996; Seidenberg & McClelland, 1987) posits learned mappings between the spellings, sounds, and meanings of words.
sentences.note: Parallel example illustrating the connectionist side; same parent [17]. Parent: [17] (contrast).

sentences.text: The theories espouse different principles of word and nonword reading, but they make the same implicit assumption that the posited mechanisms and mappings are dedicated functions of sustained structures in the brains and minds of readers.
sentences.note: Asserts a novel generalization (shared implicit assumption) across the illustrated theories, going beyond mere synthesis, so retained as primary rather than link:closing.

sentences.text: Model outputs can be shaped by task demands and context if enabled by the mechanisms or mappings, but the model architectures cannot reorganize themselves for different purposes under different conditions.
sentences.note: Unpacks "dedicated functions of sustained structures" from [20]. Parent: [20] (tempstat).

sentences.text: Assumptions of dedicated functions and sustained structures are held by most models of language of cognitive functions.
sentences.note: Asserts the breadth of the assumption defined in [20]/[21].

sentences.text: LLMs offer a different approach to theorizing about the mental lexicon, and language and cognition in general.
sentences.note: Synthesizes/transitions out of the dedicated-function discussion; nearest primary parent is [22] (immediately preceding). Parent: [22] (emph).
